(born May 16, 1960) is an American basketball executive who is the CEO of the [San Antonio Spurs](/wiki/San_Antonio_Spurs "San Antonio Spurs") of the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") (NBA). He was named general manager in 2002 after five seasons serving as team president. Buford is also the president of sports franchises for Spurs Sports & Entertainment. **Robert Canterbury Buford** (born May 16, 1960) is an American basketball executive who is the CEO of the [San Antonio Spurs](/wiki/San_Antonio_Spurs "San Antonio Spurs") of the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") (NBA). He was named general manager in 2002 after five seasons serving as team president. Buford is also the president of sports franchises for Spurs Sports & Entertainment. Buford has won the [NBA Executive of the Year](/wiki/NBA_Executive_of_the_Year_Award "NBA Executive of the Year Award") award twice, for the 2013‍–‍14 and 2015‍–‍16 seasons, before his promotion to CEO prior to the start of the 2019‍–‍20 season.

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=R._C._Buford&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Buford's father, Bob, was a successful oilman and rancher in [Wichita, Kansas](/wiki/Wichita,_Kansas "Wichita, Kansas").[[1]](#cite_note-tramel-1)

Buford spent two seasons as a walk-on for the [Texas A&M Aggies](/wiki/Texas_A%26M_Aggies_men%27s_basketball "Texas A&M Aggies men's basketball").[[1]](#cite_note-tramel-1) He transferred to the [Oklahoma State Cowboys](/wiki/Oklahoma_State_Cowboys_basketball "Oklahoma State Cowboys basketball") in 1980.[[1]](#cite_note-tramel-1) Buford sat out the 1980‍–‍81 season and then suffered eight broken ribs in a car crash, ending his playing career.[[1]](#cite_note-tramel-1)

Buford graduated from [Friends University](/wiki/Friends_University "Friends University") with a [Bachelor of Science](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Science "Bachelor of Science") in management.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Coaching career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=R._C._Buford&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Coaching career")]

Buford started his coaching career in 1983 as an assistant with the [Kansas Jayhawks](/wiki/Kansas_Jayhawks_men%27s_basketball "Kansas Jayhawks men's basketball").[[3]](#cite_note-hunt-3) He spent five seasons with the team and was a member of the coaching staff when the Jayhawks won the 1988 NCAA championship.[[3]](#cite_note-hunt-3)

Buford first joined the Spurs in the summer of 1988 as an assistant coach on [Larry Brown](/wiki/Larry_Brown_(basketball) "Larry Brown (basketball)")'s staff. He spent four seasons as an assistant with the Spurs, including the 1989‍–‍90 and 1990‍–‍91 seasons when the team captured back-to-back Midwest Division titles. In the summer of 1992, he became the number-one assistant for Brown with the [Los Angeles Clippers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Clippers "Los Angeles Clippers"). He spent one season with the Clippers before moving to the [University of Florida](/wiki/University_of_Florida "University of Florida") for the 1993‍–‍94 season.

## NBA executive career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=R._C._Buford&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: NBA executive career")]

Spurs general manager [Gregg Popovich](/wiki/Gregg_Popovich "Gregg Popovich") then hired Buford as the Spurs' head scout in the summer of 1994 and he has been with the team since that time. In the summer of 1997 he was named the Spurs' director of scouting, and then two years later in 1999 was promoted to the position of assistant general manager. He was named general manager in 2002.

Buford has won five NBA championships with the Spurs ([1999](/wiki/1998%E2%80%9399_NBA_season "1998–99 NBA season"), [2003](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_NBA_season "2002–03 NBA season"), [2005](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_NBA_season "2004–05 NBA season"), [2007](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_NBA_season "2006–07 NBA season"), [2014](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_NBA_season "2013–14 NBA season")), four as general manager ([2003](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_NBA_season "2002–03 NBA season"), [2005](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_NBA_season "2004–05 NBA season"), [2007](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_NBA_season "2006–07 NBA season"), [2014](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_NBA_season "2013–14 NBA season")).

Buford won the 2013–14 NBA Basketball Executive of the Year award on May 7, 2014,[[4]](#cite_note-4) and later won the same honor for the 2015‍–‍16 season.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

On July 23, 2019, the Spurs announced Buford will be promoted from general manager to CEO for the Spurs, effective September 3. His previous role will be filled by assistant general manager [Brian Wright](/wiki/Brian_Wright_(basketball) "Brian Wright (basketball)").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=R._C._Buford&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Personal life")]

His son, [Chase](/wiki/Chase_Buford "Chase Buford"), played basketball for the [Kansas Jayhawks](/wiki/Kansas_Jayhawks "Kansas Jayhawks") and was the head coach of the [NBL](/wiki/National_Basketball_League_(Australia) "National Basketball League (Australia)") team, the [Sydney Kings](/wiki/Sydney_Kings "Sydney Kings"). He is currently an assistant coach at Kansas. [[7]](#cite_note-7) His daughter C.C., who played golf for the [College of Charleston](/wiki/College_of_Charleston "College of Charleston"), now coaches at [East Carolina University](/wiki/East_Carolina_University "East Carolina University").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Buford met [Bill Self](/wiki/Bill_Self "Bill Self") when he served as Self's host during a recruiting visit at [Oklahoma State University](/wiki/Oklahoma_State_University "Oklahoma State University") in 1981.[[1]](#cite_note-tramel-1) Buford has called Self his best friend.[[1]](#cite_note-tramel-1)
